content.text: Object & Function Dependencies
Objects, arrays, and functions are compared by reference in JavaScript, not by value. When React evaluates dependency changes using
Object.is
, it checks whether the reference is the same pointer in memory — not whether the content is equal. A new object literal
{ size: 10 }
is a different reference than another
{ size: 10 }
even though they look identical. This distinction is the source of one of the most common
useEffect
bugs: effects that fire on every render despite nothing meaningful changing.

attrs.alt: Object identity vs value — why objects cause over-firing

content.text: Why New References Are Created on Every Render
Every time a React component renders, it executes its function body. Any value created
inside
the function body is a brand-new allocation — even if the content is identical to the previous render.
Both effects re-run on every render because
style
and
handleClick
are new references. The content may be logically identical, but React's reference comparison cannot detect that.
The Four Fixes for Unstable Object Dependencies
Fix 1: Destructure to Primitives
The simplest fix is to use the primitive values that make up the object as separate dependencies. Primitives (
string
number
boolean
) compare by value, so they only cause a re-run when they actually change.
Fix 2: useMemo to Stabilise the Object Reference
useMemo

memoises the object itself, returning the same reference as long as the inputs have not changed. Use this when the object must be constructed from multiple values that cannot be listed individually as deps, or when the object is also passed to child components.
Fix 3: Move the Object Inside the Effect
If the object is only used inside the effect and nowhere else, construct it inside the callback. The object is no longer a dependency — only the primitives that feed into it are.
Fix 4: useCallback for Function Dependencies
useCallback
does for functions what
useMemo

does for objects: it returns the same function reference as long as the listed deps have not changed. Use it when a function is passed as a prop and then used as a dependency inside the child's effect.
Functions Defined Inside the Component
A function defined inside the component body is re-created on every render. Adding it to the dependency array causes the effect to re-run on every render — the same problem as object literals.

Fix B (moving the function inside) is usually cleaner for functions that are only used by that one effect. Fix A (useCallback) is better when the function is also passed to children or used by multiple effects.
Arrays as Dependencies
Arrays follow the same reference semantics as objects. A new array literal created in the component body is a new reference on every render.
Diagnosing Over-Firing Effects
When an effect fires more often than expected, add a log inside to identify which dependency changed:
Comparing Strategies
Problem
Strategy
Trade-off
Object literal in dep
Destructure to primitives
Simplest; only works if all fields are primitive
Object literal in dep
useMemo in component body
Keeps object as dep; slightly more code
Object literal in dep
Move construction inside effect
Cleanest; only when object not used outside
Function in dep
useCallback
Stabilises fn; adds dependency on fn's own deps
Function in dep
Define function inside effect
No extra hook; best when fn only used in effect
Array in dep
Derive a stable primitive (join)
Simple; loses structural info
Array in dep
useMemo in component body
Preserves the array; stable reference
When NOT to Memoize
useMemo
and
useCallback

have their own cost: the memo value must be stored, and the dependency comparison must run on every render. For cheap objects or functions that are used only once, moving the creation inside the effect is almost always simpler and faster than memoising.
Guideline:
do not add
useMemo
or
useCallback

preemptively. First write the straightforward code, observe whether the effect over-fires, then apply the simplest fix. Destructuring to primitives or moving logic inside the effect should be tried before reaching for memo hooks.
Infinite Re-Render Loop from Unstable Dependencies

The most dangerous form of the unstable-reference problem occurs when the effect also updates state, and the state update is what passes a new object reference on the next render. The chain becomes a loop: effect runs → setState → re-render → new object ref → dep 'changes' → effect runs again → repeat.
Accessing a specific primitive property of the object (
source.url
) is almost always the right fix: you only care about the URL changing, not the object identity.
Context Values as Dependencies

Context values behave exactly like any other React value when used as a dependency. If the context provides an object and the provider creates a new object reference on every render, every consumer's effects will over-fire.
This is one of the most common React performance issues: a context that changes on every render causes all consumers (and their effects) to update, even when the relevant data has not changed.
Custom Hooks Returning Objects

When a custom hook returns a new object literal on every invocation, callers that use the returned value as an effect dependency will over-fire. Custom hooks should either return primitives, memoised objects, or individual values.
useCallback Deep Dive: Dep Chain
useCallback
does not eliminate dependencies — it moves them. The callback's own dependencies become the inputs to
useCallback
. If those inputs are unstable, the callback itself becomes unstable.

Stability must be established at every level of the dependency chain. If the root value (e.g. a prop object from the parent) is unstable, memoising derived values from it only pushes the problem one level deeper.
The useEffectEvent Pattern (React 19+)
React 19 introduces
useEffectEvent
(experimental) to address the case where you want the effect to run reactively on some deps but always read the latest version of others without adding them to the dep array.
Until
useEffectEvent
stabilises, the ref pattern achieves the same result. The key insight is to separate values you react to (deps) from values you just read (non-deps via refs).
Summary

Objects, arrays, and functions compare by reference in JavaScript. Placing them in a dependency array without stabilising their references causes effects to fire on every render, defeating the purpose of the dependency array.
Object.is compares objects and functions by reference — not by content.
A new object literal { } or array [] in the component body is a new reference on every render.
Arrow functions defined in the component body are new references on every render.
Fix for objects: destructure to primitives, or use useMemo to stabilise the reference.
Fix for functions: move definition inside the effect, or use useCallback to stabilise.
Fix for arrays: derive a stable primitive (join), or use useMemo.
Moving computation inside the effect is the simplest fix when the value is only used there.
Add useMemo/useCallback only after observing the problem — not preemptively.
